description: Complete Greek mythology pack with beings, stories, places, motifs, groups, objects, relationships, sources, and notes.

mythologies.name: Greek Mythology
mythologies.summary: The body of myths and teachings belonging to the ancient Greeks concerning their gods and heroes.
mythologies.overview: Greek mythology encompasses the cosmogony, pantheon, heroic sagas, and ritual practices of ancient Greek religion. It influenced Roman mythology and Western culture profoundly.
mythologies.worldviewSummary: Gods and humans exist in a hierarchical cosmos. Fate (Moira) governs mortals; gods are powerful but not omnipotent. The world is ordered yet capricious.
mythologies.cosmologySummary: Three realms: Olympus (gods), Earth (mortals), Underworld (Hades). Tartarus below; primordial Chaos at origin. Ocean surrounds the flat earth.

beings.name: Zeus
beings.summary: Supreme king of the Olympian gods, ruler of the cosmos and embodiment of divine authority, justice, and cosmic order. As the wielder of thunderbolts and patron of hospitality, Zeus maintains the fundamental balance between celestial power and earthly governance, while embodying the complex duality of sovereign justice and personal capriciousness that defines Greek theological understanding of divine rulership.
beings.originSummary: Youngest son of the Titans Cronus and Rhea, Zeus emerged victorious from the cosmic struggle against primordial forces, establishing the Olympian order through the Titanomachy. His rise to power represents the triumph of cosmic law over chaotic nature, establishing the divine hierarchy that would govern both celestial realms and earthly affairs. As the central figure of Greek religion, Zeus embodies the tension between supreme authority and human-like limitations, particularly in his numerous romantic entanglements and displays of wrath, which serve as cautionary tales about the proper exercise of divine power.

beings.name: Poseidon
beings.summary: Master of oceanic realms and seismic forces, Poseidon embodies the untamable power of nature and the psychological depths of the unconscious mind. As creator of horses and ruler of maritime domains, he represents both the practical necessity of sea travel and the terrifying unpredictability of natural disasters that ancient Greeks sought to understand through divine agency.

beings.name: Athena
beings.summary: Goddess of wisdom, strategic warfare, and crafts, protector of Athens. Athena guides Odysseus home through clever counsel and helps Perseus defeat Medusa with reflective shield tactics. She competes with Poseidon for patronage of Athens, winning by creating the olive tree. Athena transforms Arachne into a spider after the mortal challenges her in weaving, demonstrating both her skill and jealousy. Symbolizes the integration of wisdom with practical action and the civilizing power of strategic thinking.

beings.name: Apollo
beings.summary: Master of arts, prophecy, and civilization, Apollo embodies the Greek ideal of harmonious order through music, poetry, and rational knowledge. As both bringer of plague and healer, he represents the dual nature of divine power to both punish and restore, serving as the primary conduit between divine wisdom and human understanding.

beings.name: Hera
beings.summary: Queen of the gods, goddess of marriage and childbirth.

beings.name: Odysseus
beings.summary: King of Ithaca, hero of the Odyssey, known for cunning.

beings.name: Persephone
beings.summary: Queen of the Underworld, goddess of spring growth.

beings.name: Artemis
beings.summary: Goddess of the hunt, wilderness, and childbirth.

beings.name: Ares
beings.summary: God of war and combat, embodying the brutal and untamed aspects of battle. Ares fights alongside the Trojans in the Iliad and is wounded by Diomedes with Athena's help. He has an affair with Aphrodite, leading to public humiliation when caught in Hephaestus's net. His children Phobos and Deimos accompany him into battle, spreading fear and terror among armies. Represents the raw, chaotic nature of warfare that exists beyond strategic planning and civilized conduct.

beings.name: Aphrodite
beings.summary: Goddess of love, beauty, and desire.

beings.name: Hermes
beings.summary: Messenger god, god of commerce, thieves, and travelers.

beings.name: Demeter
beings.summary: Goddess of grain, harvest, and agriculture.

beings.name: Cronus
beings.summary: Titan king; overthrew Uranus; overthrown by Zeus.

beings.name: Rhea
beings.summary: Titaness, mother of the Olympians; saved Zeus from Cronus.

beings.name: Prometheus
beings.summary: Titan who gave fire to humanity; bound and tormented by Zeus.

beings.name: Heracles
beings.summary: Greatest Greek hero; performed the Twelve Labours.

beings.name: Achilles
beings.summary: Greatest Greek warrior; hero of the Iliad; vulnerable heel.

beings.name: Hades
beings.summary: Ruler of the underworld and judge of souls, Hades governs the realm of the dead with unwavering authority and impartial justice. As guardian of both earthly wealth (precious metals) and psychological transition, he represents the necessary boundary between life and death that maintains cosmic balance in Greek understanding of mortality.

beings.name: Hephaestus
beings.summary: God of fire, metalworking, and craftsmanship.

beings.name: Dionysus
beings.summary: God of wine, theater, and ecstasy.

beings.name: Helen of Troy
beings.summary: Most beautiful woman in Greece; cause of Trojan War.
epithets: Face that launched a thousand ships

beings.name: Orion
beings.summary: Great hunter; placed among stars after death.

beings.name: Sisyphus
beings.summary: Cunning king condemned to eternally push a boulder uphill.

beings.name: Echo
beings.summary: Nymph cursed to only repeat the words of others.

beings.name: Narcissus
beings.summary: Beautiful youth who fell in love with his own reflection.

beings.name: Philoctetes
beings.summary: Greek warrior who possessed Heracles' bow and arrows.

beings.name: Cassandra
beings.summary: Priestess cursed to prophesy truth but never be believed.

beings.name: Tiresias
beings.summary: Blind prophet of Thebes who experienced life as both man and woman.

beings.name: Medea
beings.summary: Sorceress who helped Jason obtain the Golden Fleece.

beings.name: Jason
beings.summary: Leader of the Argonauts who sought the Golden Fleece.

stories.name: Titanomachy
stories.summary: Ten-year war between the Olympian gods and the Titans.
stories.description: Zeus and his siblings, aided by the Cyclopes and Hecatoncheires, wage war against Cronus and the Titans for supremacy of the cosmos.
stories.sequenceSummary: Zeus frees siblings from Cronus; allies with imprisoned giants; decade of battle; Titans defeated and imprisoned in Tartarus.
stories.outcomeSummary: Olympians victorious; Titans imprisoned; Zeus becomes king.

stories.name: Theogony
stories.summary: Hesiod's account of the origin of the gods and the cosmos.
stories.description: Chaos gives rise to Gaia, Tartarus, Eros; Gaia bears Uranus; Cronus overthrows Uranus; Zeus overthrows Cronus.

stories.name: Abduction of Persephone
stories.summary: Hades abducts Persephone; Demeter's grief causes winter.
stories.description: Hades takes Persephone to the Underworld. Demeter searches and grieves, halting growth. Zeus negotiates; Persephone returns for part of the year, explaining seasons.
stories.outcomeSummary: Persephone spends part of year in Underworld (winter), part on earth (spring/summer).

stories.name: Odysseus' Journey Home
stories.summary: Odysseus' ten-year voyage from Troy to Ithaca.
stories.description: After the Trojan War, Odysseus faces cyclopes, sirens, Scylla, Charybdis, and Calypso before returning to reclaim his kingdom.

stories.name: Gigantomachy
stories.summary: War between the Olympians and the Giants.
stories.description: The Giants attack Olympus; the gods defeat them with the help of Heracles.

stories.name: Birth of Athena
stories.summary: Athena springs fully armed from Zeus' head.
stories.description: Zeus swallows Metis; Athena is born from his head, fully armed.

stories.name: Prometheus and Fire
stories.summary: Prometheus steals fire for humanity; punished by Zeus.
stories.description: Prometheus steals fire from the gods and gives it to humans. Zeus punishes him by chaining him to a rock where an eagle eats his liver daily.
stories.outcomeSummary: Prometheus bound; Heracles eventually frees him.

stories.name: Judgment of Paris
stories.summary: Paris awards the golden apple to Aphrodite, leading to the Trojan War.
stories.description: Paris must choose the fairest among Hera, Athena, and Aphrodite. He picks Aphrodite for the promise of Helen.

stories.name: Achilles in the Iliad
stories.summary: Achilles' wrath and the death of Hector.
stories.description: Achilles withdraws from battle after Agamemnon insults him; Patroclus dies; Achilles returns and kills Hector.

stories.name: Quest for the Golden Fleece
stories.summary: Jason and the Argonauts sail to Colchis to obtain the Golden Fleece.
stories.description: Jason assembles the Argonauts and sails the Argo to Colchis, where with Medea's help he obtains the Golden Fleece from King Aeetes.
stories.outcomeSummary: Jason obtains the fleece but later betrays Medea, leading to tragedy.

stories.name: Twelve Labors of Heracles
stories.summary: Heracles performs twelve impossible tasks to atone for killing his family.
stories.description: After killing his family in a madness sent by Hera, Heracles must complete twelve labors for King Eurystheus, including slaying the Nemean Lion and capturing Cerberus.
stories.outcomeSummary: Heracles achieves immortality through his suffering and accomplishments.

stories.name: Echo and Narcissus
stories.summary: Tragic love story of the nymph Echo and the beautiful Narcissus.
stories.description: Echo, cursed to repeat others' words, falls for Narcissus who loves only his own reflection. Both waste away and transform into natural phenomena.
stories.outcomeSummary: Echo becomes just a voice; Narcissus becomes the narcissus flower.

places.name: Mount Olympus
places.summary: Divine abode of the Olympian gods.
places.description: The highest mountain in Greece, considered the home of the twelve Olympians. A realm of eternal spring and divine feasting.

places.name: Olympus (Mountain)
places.summary: The physical mountain identified as the gods' dwelling.

places.name: Underworld (Hades)
places.summary: Realm of the dead, ruled by Hades.
places.description: Accessed via the river Styx. Contains Elysium, Tartarus, and the Asphodel Meadows. Persephone rules alongside Hades for part of the year.

places.name: Tartarus
places.summary: Deepest abyss; prison of the Titans.
places.description: The deepest region of the Underworld. Titans and other enemies are imprisoned here.

places.name: Elysium
places.description: The Fields of Elysium; a blessed afterlife for the righteous and heroes.

places.name: River Styx
places.summary: River of the Underworld; boundary of the dead.
places.description: The river that forms the boundary of the Underworld. Oaths sworn by the Styx are unbreakable.

places.name: Delphi
places.summary: Site of Apollo's oracle, the navel of the world.
places.description: The Pythia delivered prophecies in a trance. "Know thyself" and "Nothing in excess" were inscribed at the temple.

places.name: Ithaca
places.summary: Home island of Odysseus, destination of his long journey.
places.description: Small island kingdom in the Ionian Sea. The homeland of Odysseus and his family, representing the ultimate goal of homecoming.

places.name: Colchis
places.summary: Eastern kingdom where the Golden Fleece was kept.
places.description: Far eastern kingdom on the Black Sea, ruled by King Aeetes. Home of Medea and guardian of the Golden Fleece.

places.name: Thebes
places.summary: Major city of central Greece, home of Oedipus and Tiresias.
places.description: Powerful city-state in Boeotia. Setting for the Oedipus cycle and many other tragic stories involving prophecies and family curses.

places.name: River Lethe
places.summary: River of forgetfulness in the Underworld.
places.description: One of the five rivers of the Underworld. Souls drink from it to forget their past lives before reincarnation.

motifs.name: Hubris and Nemesis
motifs.summary: Excessive pride leading to divine punishment.
motifs.description: Characters who overstep mortal limits or defy the gods incur nemesis—retribution that restores cosmic order.
motifs.interpretation: Central to Greek ethics; defines the boundary between human and divine.

motifs.name: Katabasis (Descent to Underworld)
motifs.summary: Heroic descent into the realm of the dead.
motifs.description: Living heroes journey to the Underworld and return, often gaining knowledge or completing a quest.
motifs.interpretation: Symbolizes transformation, confronting death, and returning with wisdom.

motifs.name: Xenia (Guest-Friendship)
motifs.summary: Sacred obligation of hospitality between host and guest.
motifs.description: Zeus Xenios protects travelers. Hosts must offer food, shelter, and gifts; guests must not overstay or harm the host.
motifs.interpretation: Foundation of Greek social order; violations lead to divine wrath.

motifs.name: Binding of the Monster
motifs.summary: Dangerous beings bound or imprisoned until the end times.
motifs.description: Fenrir, Prometheus, the Titans—forces of chaos are contained but prophesied to break free.
motifs.interpretation: Order is temporary; containment implies eventual release.

groups.name: Olympians
groups.summary: The twelve principal gods of the Greek pantheon.

groups.name: Titans
groups.summary: The generation of gods preceding the Olympians.

objects.name: Zeus' Thunderbolt
objects.summary: Divine weapon of Zeus, symbol of his power.

objects.name: Poseidon's Trident
objects.summary: Three-pronged spear, symbol of sea power.
objects.symbolicMeaning: Mastery of the sea, earth-shaking power

objects.name: Aegis
objects.summary: Shield or breastplate with Medusa's head.

sources.title: Theogony
sources.description: Poetic account of the origin of the gods and cosmos.
sources.citation: Hesiod. Theogony. Trans. M.L. West. Oxford, 1966.

sources.title: Odyssey
sources.description: Epic of Odysseus' return from Troy.
sources.citation: Homer. Odyssey. Trans. R. Fagles. Penguin, 1996.

sources.title: Iliad
sources.description: Epic of the Trojan War and Achilles' wrath.
sources.citation: Homer. Iliad. Trans. R. Fagles. Penguin, 1990.

sources.title: Bibliotheca
sources.citation: Apollodorus. The Library. Trans. J.G. Frazer. Loeb, 1921.

sources.title: Homeric Hymns
sources.description: Collection of hymns to individual gods.

sources.title: Metamorphoses
sources.description: Roman poetic retelling of Greek myths, focusing on transformations.
sources.citation: Ovid. Metamorphoses. Trans. A.D. Melville. Oxford, 1986.
sources.notes: Important source for many Greek myths, though Roman interpretation.

sources.title: Argonautica
sources.description: Epic poem telling the story of Jason and the Argonauts.
sources.citation: Apollonius of Rhodes. Argonautica. Trans. R. Hunter. Oxford, 1993.
sources.notes: Main source for the Golden Fleece quest.

notes.title: Katabasis in Odyssey
notes.content: Odysseus' descent to consult Tiresias fits the katabasis motif: living hero enters underworld, gains knowledge, returns. Compare Aeneid, Gilgamesh.

notes.title: Binding motif: Greek vs Norse
notes.content: Prometheus bound (Greek) vs Fenrir bound (Norse). Both: dangerous figure contained, prophesied to break free. Containment as temporary order.
